patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs_log_dev_io_error() function to log device I/O errors for all bio error status codes, not just BLK_STS_IOERR and BLK_STS_TARGET. This change is intended to prevent incorrect conclusions about system behavior when encountering unexpected bio failure status values.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with a Reviewed-by tag, indicating it meets code quality standards and is suitable for merge.
review_comments.raw_body:
\u5728 2026/3/14 05:56, Boris Burkov \u5199\u9053:
> As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> 
> For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().
> 
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>

Reviewed-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>

Thanks,
Qu


> ---
>   fs/btrfs/bio.c | 3 +--
>   1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 2 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/bio.c b/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> index 2a2a21aec817..08b1d62603d0 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> @@ -352,8 +352,7 @@ static void btrfs_log_dev_io_error(const struct bio *bio, struct btrfs_device *d
>   {
>   	if (!dev || !dev->bdev)
>   		return;
> -	if (bio->bi_status != BLK_STS_IOERR && bio->bi_status != BLK_STS_TARGET)
> -		return;
> +	ASSERT(bio->bi_status);
>   
>   	if (btrfs_op(bio) == BTRFS_MAP_WRITE)
>   		btrfs_dev_stat_inc_and_print(dev, BTRFS_DEV_STAT_WRITE_ERRS);

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/2] vfs: follow-on fixes for i_ino widening

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/2] vfs: follow-on fixes for i_ino widening
[PATCH v2 0/2] vfs: follow-on fixes for i_ino widening

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/2] nilfs2: fix 64-bit division operations in nilfs_bmap_find_target_in_group()
series_items.patch_summary: The patch fixes a build failure on 32-bit ARM caused by the introduction of 64-bit inode numbers, which triggers undefined behavior in the `__aeabi_uldivmod` function. The fix involves using 64-bit division interfaces to calculate the group number and index within that group.

review_comments.author: David Laight
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns that `entries_per_group` might not be a 64-bit value and suggested using a simpler approach to calculate the index within the group, which would also improve performance.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 13 Mar 2026 14:45:20 -0400
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org> wrote:

> With the change to make inode->i_ino a u64, the build started failing on
> 32-bit ARM with:
> 
>     ERROR: modpost: "__aeabi_uldivmod" [fs/nilfs2/nilfs2.ko] undefined!
> 
> Fix this by using the 64-bit division interfaces in
> nilfs_bmap_find_target_in_group().
> 
> Fixes: 998a59d371c2 ("treewide: fix missed i_ino format specifier conversions")
> Reported-by: kernel test robot <lkp@intel.com>
> Closes: https://lore.kernel.org/oe-kbuild-all/202603100602.KPxiClIO-lkp@intel.com/
> Reviewed-by: Viacheslav Dubeyko <slava@dubeyko.com>
> Acked-by: Ryusuke Konishi <konishi.ryusuke@gmail.com>
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  fs/nilfs2/bmap.c | 9 ++++++---
>  1 file changed, 6 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/nilfs2/bmap.c b/fs/nilfs2/bmap.c
> index 824f2bd91c167965ec3a660202b6e6c5f1fe007e..abcf5252578ad24f694bfccf525893674bfcb4bc 100644
> --- a/fs/nilfs2/bmap.c
> +++ b/fs/nilfs2/bmap.c
> @@ -455,11 +455,14 @@ __u64 nilfs_bmap_find_target_in_group(const struct nilfs_bmap *bmap)
>  {
>  	struct inode *dat = nilfs_bmap_get_dat(bmap);
>  	unsigned long entries_per_group = nilfs_palloc_entries_per_group(dat);
> -	unsigned long group = bmap->b_inode->i_ino / entries_per_group;

Are you sure entries_per_group can be more than 32 bits?
It looks like something that will be the same size on 32 and 64bit.

> +	unsigned long group;
> +	u32 index;
> +
> +	group = div_u64(bmap->b_inode->i_ino, entries_per_group);

You don't need the full 64 by 64 divide.
IIRC there are both div_u64_u32() and div_u64_ulong().

> +	div_u64_rem(bmap->b_inode->i_ino, NILFS_BMAP_GROUP_DIV, &index);

NILFD_BMAP_GROUP_DIV is 8 (and probably has to be a power of 2).
So:
	index = bmap->b_inode->i_ino & (NILFS_BMAP_GROUP_DIV - 1);
is the same and likely much faster to calculate.
(The compiler will have done that optimisation before.)

	David


>  
>  	return group * entries_per_group +
> -		(bmap->b_inode->i_ino % NILFS_BMAP_GROUP_DIV) *
> -		(entries_per_group / NILFS_BMAP_GROUP_DIV);
> +	       index * (entries_per_group / NILFS_BMAP_GROUP_DIV);
>  }
>  
>  static struct lock_class_key nilfs_bmap_dat_lock_key;
>

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged David's concerns and suggested that the NILFS2 maintainers should provide guidance on how to proceed with the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Sat, 2026-03-14 at 12:47 +0000, David Laight wrote:
> On Fri, 13 Mar 2026 14:45:20 -0400
> Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org> wrote:
> 
> > With the change to make inode->i_ino a u64, the build started failing on
> > 32-bit ARM with:
> > 
> >     ERROR: modpost: "__aeabi_uldivmod" [fs/nilfs2/nilfs2.ko] undefined!
> > 
> > Fix this by using the 64-bit division interfaces in
> > nilfs_bmap_find_target_in_group().
> > 
> > Fixes: 998a59d371c2 ("treewide: fix missed i_ino format specifier conversions")
> > Reported-by: kernel test robot <lkp@intel.com>
> > Closes: https://lore.kernel.org/oe-kbuild-all/202603100602.KPxiClIO-lkp@intel.com/
> > Reviewed-by: Viacheslav Dubeyko <slava@dubeyko.com>
> > Acked-by: Ryusuke Konishi <konishi.ryusuke@gmail.com>
> > Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> > ---
> >  fs/nilfs2/bmap.c | 9 ++++++---
> >  1 file changed, 6 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)
> > 
> > diff --git a/fs/nilfs2/bmap.c b/fs/nilfs2/bmap.c
> > index 824f2bd91c167965ec3a660202b6e6c5f1fe007e..abcf5252578ad24f694bfccf525893674bfcb4bc 100644
> > --- a/fs/nilfs2/bmap.c
> > +++ b/fs/nilfs2/bmap.c
> > @@ -455,11 +455,14 @@ __u64 nilfs_bmap_find_target_in_group(const struct nilfs_bmap *bmap)
> >  {
> >  	struct inode *dat = nilfs_bmap_get_dat(bmap);
> >  	unsigned long entries_per_group = nilfs_palloc_entries_per_group(dat);
> > -	unsigned long group = bmap->b_inode->i_ino / entries_per_group;
> 
> Are you sure entries_per_group can be more than 32 bits?
> It looks like something that will be the same size on 32 and 64bit.
> 

I'm not sure of anything here. I'm just want to get this to compile on
all arches. FWIW, I'm not looking to optimize anything in this patch.

> > +	unsigned long group;
> > +	u32 index;
> > +
> > +	group = div_u64(bmap->b_inode->i_ino, entries_per_group);
> 
> You don't need the full 64 by 64 divide.
> IIRC there are both div_u64_u32() and div_u64_ulong().
>
> > +	div_u64_rem(bmap->b_inode->i_ino, NILFS_BMAP_GROUP_DIV, &index);
> 
> NILFD_BMAP_GROUP_DIV is 8 (and probably has to be a power of 2).
> So:
> 	index = bmap->b_inode->i_ino & (NILFS_BMAP_GROUP_DIV - 1);
> is the same and likely much faster to calculate.
> (The compiler will have done that optimisation before.)
> 
> 

That all sounds reasonable to me. At this point though, it would be
better if the NILFS2 folks stepped in with how they'd prefer this be
done.

> 
> >  
> >  	return group * entries_per_group +
> > -		(bmap->b_inode->i_ino % NILFS_BMAP_GROUP_DIV) *
> > -		(entries_per_group / NILFS_BMAP_GROUP_DIV);
> > +	       index * (entries_per_group / NILFS_BMAP_GROUP_DIV);
> >  }
> >  
> >  static struct lock_class_key nilfs_bmap_dat_lock_key;
> > 

-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] EVM: add comment describing why ino field is still unsigned long
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a comment to the EVM code explaining why the inode number field remains an unsigned long, allowing existing HMAC and signatures from 32-bit hosts to continue working when the inode hasn't changed and fits in a u32.

review_comments.author: Mimi Zohar
review_comments.summary: Expressed gratitude for Jeff Layton's patch and applied the Reviewed-by tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 2026-03-13 at 14:45 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> Mimi pointed out that we didn't widen the inode number field in struct
> h_misc alongside the inode->i_ino widening. While we could make an
> equivalent change there, that would require EVM resigning on all 32-bit
> hosts.
> 
> Instead, leave the field as an unsigned long. This should have no effect
> on 64-bit hosts, and allow things to continue working on 32-bit hosts in
> the cases where the i_ino fits in 32-bits.
> 
> Add a comment explaining why it's being left as unsigned long.
> 
> Cc: Mimi Zohar <zohar@linux.ibm.com>
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

Thanks, Jeff.

Reviewed-by: Mimi Zohar <zohar@linux.ibm.com>


> ---
>  security/integrity/evm/evm_crypto.c | 6 ++++++
>  1 file changed, 6 insertions(+)
> 
> diff --git a/security/integrity/evm/evm_crypto.c b/security/integrity/evm/evm_crypto.c
> index c0ca4eedb0fe5d5c30f45f515a4bc90248ec64ea..1c41af2f91a60a714878ff93b554c90e45546503 100644
> --- a/security/integrity/evm/evm_crypto.c
> +++ b/security/integrity/evm/evm_crypto.c
> @@ -144,6 +144,12 @@ static void hmac_add_misc(struct shash_desc *desc, struct inode *inode,
>  			  char type, char *digest)
>  {
>  	struct h_misc {
> +		/*
> +		 * Although inode->i_ino is now u64, this field remains
> +		 * unsigned long to allow existing HMAC and signatures from
> +		 * 32-bit hosts to continue working when i_ino hasn't changed
> +		 * and fits in a u32.
> +		 */
>  		unsigned long ino;
>  		__u32 generation;
>  		uid_t uid;

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses follow-on issues related to inode->i_ino widening, specifically fixing division operations in nilfs2 and adding a comment for EVM. The patches aim to ensure compatibility across different architectures.

review_comments.author: David Laight
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the division operation in nilfs2, suggesting a simpler approach using div_u64_u32() and optimizing the calculation of index. Also pointed out that entries_per_group should not exceed 32 bits.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 13 Mar 2026 14:45:20 -0400
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org> wrote:

> With the change to make inode->i_ino a u64, the build started failing on
> 32-bit ARM with:
> 
>     ERROR: modpost: "__aeabi_uldivmod" [fs/nilfs2/nilfs2.ko] undefined!

> Fix this by using the 64-bit division interfaces in
> nilfs_bmap_find_target_in_group().
> 
> Fixes: 998a59d371c2 ("treewide: fix missed i_ino format specifier conversions")
> Reported-by: kernel test robot <lkp@intel.com>
> Closes: https://lore.kernel.org/oe-kbuild-all/202603100602.KPxiClIO-lkp@intel.com/
> Reviewed-by: Viacheslav Dubeyko <slava@dubeyko.com>
> Acked-by: Ryusuke Konishi <konishi.ryusuke@gmail.com>
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  fs/nilfs2/bmap.c | 9 ++++++---
>  1 file changed, 6 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/nilfs2/bmap.c b/fs/nilfs2/bmap.c
> index 824f2bd91c167965ec3a660202b6e6c5f1fe007e..abcf5252578ad24f694bfccf525893674bfcb4bc 100644
> --- a/fs/nilfs2/bmap.c
> +++ b/fs/nilfs2/bmap.c
> @@ -455,11 +455,14 @@ __u64 nilfs_bmap_find_target_in_group(const struct nilfs_bmap *bmap)
>  {
>  	struct inode *dat = nilfs_bmap_get_dat(bmap);
>  	unsigned long entries_per_group = nilfs_palloc_entries_per_group(dat);
> -	unsigned long group = bmap->b_inode->i_ino / entries_per_group;

Are you sure entries_per_group can be more than 32 bits?
It looks like something that will be the same size on 32 and 64bit.

> +	unsigned long group;
> +	u32 index;
> +
> +	group = div_u64(bmap->b_inode->i_ino, entries_per_group);

You don't need the full 64 by 64 divide.
IIRC there are both div_u64_u32() and div_u64_ulong().

> +	div_u64_rem(bmap->b_inode->i_ino, NILFS_BMAP_GROUP_DIV, &index);

NILFD_BMAP_GROUP_DIV is 8 (and probably has to be a power of 2).
So:
	index = bmap->b_inode->i_ino & (NILFS_BMAP_GROUP_DIV - 1);
is the same and likely much faster to calculate.
(The compiler will have done that optimisation before.)

	David


>  
>  	return group * entries_per_group +
> -		(bmap->b_inode->i_ino % NILFS_BMAP_GROUP_DIV) *
> -		(entries_per_group / NILFS_BMAP_GROUP_DIV);
> +	       index * (entries_per_group / NILFS_BMAP_GROUP_DIV);
>  }
>  
>  static struct lock_class_key nilfs_bmap_dat_lock_key;
>

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2 1/2] nilfs2: fix 64-bit division operations in nilfs_bmap_find_target_in_group()
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Patch author seeks review and potential input from NILFS2 maintainers for a patch fixing 64-bit division operations in nilfs_bmap_find_target_in_group().

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Author seeks review and input from NILFS2 maintainers, but expresses uncertainty about the patch's correctness and suggests deferring to them for guidance.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v7 2/2] NFSD: convert callback RPC program to per-net namespace
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch simplifies the NFSD callback RPC program by embedding its struct directly within the nfsd_net structure, eliminating the need for a separate allocation.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that embedding the nfsd_cb struct inside nfsd_net would create a tight coupling between netns.h and the NFSv4 callback program, which he avoided by using a pointer to hide implementation details.
review_comments.raw_body:
The reason I did this is because otherwise netns.h would need
to have knowledge of the details of the NFSv4 callback program.
Things like the NFSv4 callback program's RPC program number
are implementation-defined...

I'm avoiding a weak layering violation by hiding those details
behind a pointer.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that embedding nfsd_cb inside nfsd_net would have been a better approach, but considers it a minor issue and does not plan to restructure the code in this patch version.
review_comments.raw_body:
Meh. I'd probably have prioritized avoiding the extra allocation, but
that's a minor thing.

Reviewed-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3] mm: migrate: requeue destination folio on deferred split queue

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm: migrate: requeue destination folio on deferred split queue
[PATCH v2] mm: migrate: requeue destination folio on deferred split queue
[PATCH v3] mm: migrate: requeue destination folio on deferred split queue

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Linux kernel's folio migration code by re-queuing the destination folio on the deferred split queue after a successful migration. The source folio is removed from the queue, but the destination folio remains and can cause underutilized THPs to escape the shrinker. The patch records whether the source folio was on the deferred split queue and its partially mapped state before unqueuing it, and re-queues the destination folio if necessary. This ensures that fully mapped but potentially underused folios are correctly handled.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by

review_comments.author: SeongJae Park
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer questioned whether the patch's changes are still relevant after being merged in Linux 6.12 and suggested adding a CC to the stable mailing list if there is user impact on THP-shrinker enabled systems.

Reviewer noted that the requeueing of the destination folio on the deferred split queue is not sufficient, and suggested checking if the source folio was partially mapped before calling deferred_split_folio()
review_comments.raw_body:
Seems the commit is merged in 6.12.  And I assume the user impact on
THP-shrinker enabled systems is visible.  If so, should we Cc stable@ ?

---

Acked-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>


Thanks,
SJ

[...]

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton requested more information about the user impact of this patch and noted that the changelog lacks specific details.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think the user impact should be visible to backport, but the
changelog is elusive on details?

review_comments.author: Wei Yang
review_comments.summary: Gave Reviewed-by

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the destination THPs were being removed from the deferred split queue during migration, and explained that this patch adds them back to the list after migration to meet user expectations.
review_comments.raw_body:
The original patches added THPs to deferred_list at fault/collapse, they
got removed but not added back to the list after migration.
This patch adds them to the deferred_list on migration. The user would
not expect the THPs to get removed from deferred_list on migration, so
this fixes user expectations.

I have CC-ed stable@vger.kernel.org to this email. Should I resend the patch
with CC stable in commit message?

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton questioned whether the patch provides a tangible benefit to userspace, suggesting that if no benefits can be identified, it may not meet the criteria for backporting.
review_comments.raw_body:
Maybe users just won't notice?

If we can't identify any benefit to userspace then I don't think this
patch meets the criteria for backporting.

review_comments.author: SeongJae Park
review_comments.summary: Reviewer SeongJae Park noted that the patch's fix may lead to underutilized THPs being split by the shrinker, resulting in users seeing less free memory than before, and requested Usama to correct his understanding.
review_comments.raw_body:
My guess of the user-visible consequence was like following.  Because THPs are
removed from the deferred_list, THP shinker cannot split the underutilized THPs
in time.  As a result, users will show less free memory than before.  I believe
I might be wrong and Usama can correct me in the case.


Thanks,
SJ

[...]

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton approved the patch, stating that it will be included in the next stable release and does not need to be rushed into mainline.
review_comments.raw_body:
That'll do, thanks ;)

Pasted, added cc:stable.  It's been there since 6.12 so I don't see a
need to rush this in, so I won't move this into mm-hotfixes - it'll go
into mainline for 7.1-rc1 after which -stable should pick it up.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/4] arm64/mm: contpte-sized exec folios for 16K and 64K pages

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/4] arm64/mm: contpte-sized exec folios for 16K and 64K pages

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/4] arm64: request contpte-sized folios for exec memory
series_items.patch_summary: The patch modifies the exec_folio_order() function to request contpte-sized folios for executable memory on arm64 architecture, optimizing iTLB entries and TLB coalescing.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: The patch is a good optimization, but the author should consider adding more comments to explain the changes and their impact on iTLB entries and TLB coalescing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/4] mm: bypass mmap_miss heuristic for VM_EXEC readahead
series_items.patch_summary: The patch modifies the do_sync_mmap_readahead() function to bypass the mmap_miss heuristic for VM_EXEC readahead. This change allows arch-preferred folio orders to be requested for executable memory, which can improve performance on certain architectures.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the patch, suggesting that it may not be necessary to move the VM_EXEC readahead block above the mmap_miss check. Proposed an alternative solution and asked for further discussion.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/4] elf: align ET_DYN base to exec folio order for contpte mapping
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adjusts the alignment of ELF binaries (ET_DYN) to ensure that their load address is aligned to the size of large folios allocated for execution memory. This is necessary because some architectures, such as arm64, require a specific alignment for contpte mapping to work correctly. Without this adjustment, the readahead patches cannot benefit from contpte mapping, leading to reduced iTLB coalescing benefits. The patch introduces a new calculation for ELF alignment based on the arch's requested folio size, ensuring that virtual addresses of exec pages are aligned to the folio boundary.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author is addressing a concern about the alignment of ET_DYN base to exec folio order for contpte mapping, specifically on ARM architecture where 64K page size is used. They explain that they chose to align with 2M (cont-pte size) to get both page fault benefit and iTLB coverage, which is already the case on x86.

The author is addressing a concern that the patch only checks for alignment when there's at least one PT_LOAD segment large enough to contain a full folio, and proposes to check all segments with PT_LOAD instead. The author agrees that this makes sense and suggests applying the change over the entire series.
review_comments.raw_body:
For us it came about because we use 64K page size on ARM, and none of the
text sections were getting hugified (because PMD size is 512M). I went with
exec_folio_order() = cont-pte size (2M) for 16K and 64K as we can get both page
fault benefit (which might not be that important) and iTLB coverage (due to
cont-pte).
x86 already faults in at 2M (HPAGE_PMD_ORDER) due to force_thp_readahead path in
do_sync_mmap_readahead() so the memory pressure introduced in ARM won't be worse
than what already exists in x86.

---

Yes I think this makes sense!

Although maybe we should check all segments with PT_LOAD. So maybe something
like below over this series?

diff --git a/fs/binfmt_elf.c b/fs/binfmt_elf.c
index 2d2b3e9fd474f..a0e83b541a7d8 100644
--- a/fs/binfmt_elf.c
+++ b/fs/binfmt_elf.c
@@ -1116,10 +1116,30 @@ static int load_elf_binary(struct linux_binprm *bprm)
                         * the hardware cannot coalesce PTEs (e.g. arm64
                         * contpte) even though the physical memory and
                         * file offset are correctly aligned.
+                        *
+                        * Only increase alignment when at least one
+                        * PT_LOAD segment is large enough to contain a
+                        * full folio and has its file offset and virtual
+                        * address folio-aligned. This avoids reducing
+                        * ASLR entropy for small binaries that cannot
+                        * benefit from contpte mapping.
                         */
-                       if (exec_folio_order())
-                               alignment = max(alignment,
-                                       (unsigned long)PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order());
+                       if (exec_folio_order()) {
+                               unsigned long folio_sz = PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order();
+
+                               for (i = 0; i < elf_ex->e_phnum; i++) {
+                                       if (elf_phdata[i].p_type != PT_LOAD)
+                                               continue;
+                                       if (elf_phdata[i].p_filesz < folio_sz)
+                                               continue;
+                                       if (!IS_ALIGNED(elf_phdata[i].p_vaddr, folio_sz))
+                                               continue;
+                                       if (!IS_ALIGNED(elf_phdata[i].p_offset, folio_sz))
+                                               continue;
+                                       alignment = max(alignment, folio_sz);
+                                       break;
+                               }
+                       }
 
                        /**
                         * DOC: PIE handling

review_comments.author: hev
review_comments.summary: Reviewer hev suggested moving the logic for aligning ET_DYN base to exec folio order into a separate function called should_align_to_exec_folio(), and further proposed that this new function be placed within the maximum_alignment() function to avoid duplicate segment walks.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think this logic should live in maximum_alignment(), so we don't
have to walk the segments twice. It might be better to move it into a
separate helper, something like should_align_to_exec_folio()?

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/4] mm: align file-backed mmap to exec folio order in thp_get_unmapped_area
series_items.patch_summary: The patch introduces a fallback alignment mechanism in thp_get_unmapped_area() to ensure that file-backed mappings, including executable segments, can be contpte-mapped when large folios are used. This is achieved by trying PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order() alignment when PMD_SIZE alignment fails.

review_comments.author: WANG Rui
review_comments.summary: Identified an issue where even when the code segment of a user-space shared library satisfies PMD_SIZE, it still doesn't end up at a PMD-aligned virtual address. Suggested skipping the PMD_SIZE == folio_sz case to avoid calling __thp_get_unmapped_area() twice with the same parameters.
review_comments.raw_body:
> +	if (exec_folio_order()) {
> +		unsigned long folio_size = PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order();
> +
> +		ret = __thp_get_unmapped_area(filp, addr, len, off, flags,
> +					      folio_size, vm_flags);
> +		if (ret)
> +			return ret;
> +	}
> +

I noticed that even when the code segment of a user-space shared library
satisfies PMD_SIZE (32MB), it still doesn\u2019t end up at a PMD-aligned virtual
address. This might be the fallback you mentioned. Adjusting p_align in the
ld.so ELF loader does work, though it also avoids extremely large PMD_SIZE
values (capped at \u226432M).

It would probably be better to skip the PMD_SIZE == folio_sz case here,
so we don\u2019t end up calling __thp_get_unmapped_area() twice with the same
parameters.

Thanks,
Rui

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses issues with contpte-sized exec folios on arm64, specifically for 16K and 64K page configurations. The first issue is that the current implementation returns an incorrect order for these page sizes, which is fixed by using a different calculation. The second issue is that readahead is disabled after 100 page faults due to the mmap_miss heuristic, but this only happens on 64K pages because the decrement paths are inactive. This is fixed by moving the VM_EXEC readahead block above the mmap_miss check. Finally, the patch ensures that the virtual address is aligned to CONT_PTE_SIZE (2M) for contpte mapping, which requires alignment of the VMA start address.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David questioned the assumption that a single order is sufficient for contpte mapping, suggesting instead to make decisions based on the mapping size.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm curious: is a single order really what we want?

I'd instead assume that we might want to make decisions based on the
mapping size.

Assume you have a 128M mapping, wouldn't we want to use a different
alignment than, say, for a 1M mapping, a 128K mapping or a 8k mapping?

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: WANG Rui
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer WANG Rui noted that rustc's performance regressed after a binutils upgrade and is working on making ld.so aware of THP, which would adjust PT_LOAD alignment to increase the chances of shared libraries being mapped by THP. He mentioned doing similar work in the kernel for executables.

Reviewer WANG Rui suggested adding constraints to increase p_align only when the segment length, virtual address, and file offset are all huge-aligned, citing a reduction in ASLR entropy as motivation.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Usama,

Glad to see you're pushing on this, I'm also following it. I first noticed this when rustc's perf regressed after a binutils upgrade. I'm trying to make ld.so to aware THP and adjust PT_LOAD alignment to increase the chances of shared libraries being mapped by THP [1]. As you're probably seen, I'm doing something similar in the kernel to improve it for executables [2].

---

I\u2019m curious, does it make sense to add some constraints here, like only increasing p_align when the segment length, virtual address, and file offset are all huge-aligned, as I did in my patch? This has come up several times in the glibc review, where increasing alignment was noted to reduce ASLR entropy.

[1] https://sourceware.org/pipermail/libc-alpha/2026-March/175776.html
[2] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/20260313005211.882831-1-r@hev.cc

Thanks,
Rui

review_comments.author: Ryan Roberts
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Ryan Roberts expressed concern about potential read amplification due to large default alignment of 2M, which may lead to unnecessary memory reads on systems with smaller page sizes like Android's 16K config.

Reviewer Ryan Roberts questioned whether it is feasible to influence ELF file alignment from the kernel, pointing out that the text section may not be the first section in the file and its alignment depends on the dynamic loader.

Reviewer Ryan Roberts suggested aligning the text section to 2M and using MADV_HUGEPAGE to enable contpte mapping, instead of relying on readahead.
review_comments.raw_body:
This was deliberate, although perhaps a bit conservative. I was concerned about
the possibility of read amplification; pointlessly reading in a load of memory
that never actually gets used. And that is independent of page size.

2M seems quite big as a default IMHO, I could imagine Android might complain
about memory pressure in their 16K config, for example.

Additionally, ELF files are normally only aligned to 64K and you can only get
the TLB benefits if the memory is aligned in physical and virtual memory.

---

I don't see how you can reliably influence this from the kernel? The ELF file
alignment is, by default, 64K (16K on Android) and there is no guarrantee that
the text section is the first section in the file. You need to align the start
of the text section to the 2M boundary and to do that, you'll need to align the
start of the file to some 64K boundary at a specific offset to the 2M boundary,
based on the size of any sections before the text section. That's a job for the
dynamic loader I think? Perhaps I've misunderstood what you're doing...

---

I think the proper way to do this is to link the text section with 2M alignment
and have the dynamic linker mark the region with MADV_HUGEPAGE?

Thanks,
Ryan

review_comments.author: hev
review_comments.summary: Reviewer hev ran benchmarks on RK3399 and observed a significant reduction in iTLB misses after applying the patch, but questioned whether it would be better to align the virtual address to PMD size instead of CONT_PTE_SIZE.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: WANG Rui <r@hev.cc>

Hi,

I ran a quick bench on RK3399:

Binutils: 2.46
GCC: 15.2.1 (--enable-host-pie)

Workload: building vmlinux from Linux v7.0-rc1 with allnoconfig.

                Base                 Patchset [1]         Patchset [2]
instructions    3,115,852,636,773    3,194,533,947,809    3,235,417,205,947
cpu-cycles      8,374,429,970,450    8,457,398,871,141    8,323,881,987,768
itlb-misses         9,250,336,037        8,033,415,293        2,946,152,935
                                             ~ -13.16%            ~ -68.15%
time elapsed             610.51 s             605.12 s             593.83 s

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260310145406.3073394-1-usama.arif@linux.dev
[2] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/20260313005211.882831-1-r@hev.cc

Should we prefer to PMD aligned here?

Thanks,
Rui

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author is addressing a concern about the alignment of virtual addresses for contpte mapping on 64K pages, explaining that they chose to use exec_folio_order() = cont-pte size (2M) because it provides both page fault benefit and iTLB coverage. They also mention that x86 already faults in at 2M due to force_thp_readahead path.

The author is addressing a concern that the patch may not correctly align virtual addresses for contpte mapping on 64K pages, which requires alignment to CONT_PTE_SIZE (2M). The author agrees that checking all segments with PT_LOAD in load_elf_binary() is necessary and proposes adding a loop to check each segment's file offset and virtual address alignment.
review_comments.raw_body:
For us it came about because we use 64K page size on ARM, and none of the
text sections were getting hugified (because PMD size is 512M). I went with
exec_folio_order() = cont-pte size (2M) for 16K and 64K as we can get both page
fault benefit (which might not be that important) and iTLB coverage (due to
cont-pte).
x86 already faults in at 2M (HPAGE_PMD_ORDER) due to force_thp_readahead path in
do_sync_mmap_readahead() so the memory pressure introduced in ARM won't be worse
than what already exists in x86.

---

Yes I think this makes sense!

Although maybe we should check all segments with PT_LOAD. So maybe something
like below over this series?

diff --git a/fs/binfmt_elf.c b/fs/binfmt_elf.c
index 2d2b3e9fd474f..a0e83b541a7d8 100644
--- a/fs/binfmt_elf.c
+++ b/fs/binfmt_elf.c
@@ -1116,10 +1116,30 @@ static int load_elf_binary(struct linux_binprm *bprm)
                         * the hardware cannot coalesce PTEs (e.g. arm64
                         * contpte) even though the physical memory and
                         * file offset are correctly aligned.
+                        *
+                        * Only increase alignment when at least one
+                        * PT_LOAD segment is large enough to contain a
+                        * full folio and has its file offset and virtual
+                        * address folio-aligned. This avoids reducing
+                        * ASLR entropy for small binaries that cannot
+                        * benefit from contpte mapping.
                         */
-                       if (exec_folio_order())
-                               alignment = max(alignment,
-                                       (unsigned long)PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order());
+                       if (exec_folio_order()) {
+                               unsigned long folio_sz = PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order();
+
+                               for (i = 0; i < elf_ex->e_phnum; i++) {
+                                       if (elf_phdata[i].p_type != PT_LOAD)
+                                               continue;
+                                       if (elf_phdata[i].p_filesz < folio_sz)
+                                               continue;
+                                       if (!IS_ALIGNED(elf_phdata[i].p_vaddr, folio_sz))
+                                               continue;
+                                       if (!IS_ALIGNED(elf_phdata[i].p_offset, folio_sz))
+                                               continue;
+                                       alignment = max(alignment, folio_sz);
+                                       break;
+                               }
+                       }
 
                        /**
                         * DOC: PIE handling

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledges that page faults are not a significant issue for the contpte feature, but emphasizes that iTLB coverage is the primary benefit. They explain their reasoning behind choosing 2M alignment and argue that adding intermediate sizes would add complexity without providing additional TLB coalescing.
review_comments.raw_body:
So I see 2 benefits from this. Page fault and iTLB coverage. IMHO page
faults are not that big of a deal? If the text section is hot, it wont
get flushed after faulting in. So the real benefit comes from improved
iTLB coverage.

For a 128M mapping, 2M alignment gives 64 contpte entries. Aligning
to something larger (say 128M) wouldn't give any additional TLB
coalescing, each 2M-aligned region independently qualifies for contpte.

Mappings smaller than 2M can't benefit from contpte regardless of
alignment, so falling back to PAGE_SIZE would be the optimal behaviour.
Adding intermediate sizes (e.g. 512K, 128K) wouldn't map to any
hardware boundary and adds complexity without TLB benefit?

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed concerns about memory pressure due to contpte mapping on 16K and 64K pages by comparing it to x86's behavior and explaining that the readahead is clamped to VMA boundaries, reducing potential memory pressure. The author does not agree with the reviewer's implication that the current code would lead to worse memory pressure.

The author acknowledges that the virtual address of shared libraries may not be aligned to the contpte size due to the get_unmapped_area callback trying PMD_SIZE alignment first, and explains how patch 4 addresses this issue by adding a fallback for folio-size alignment.

Author acknowledged that the force_thp_readahead path triggered by MADV_HUGEPAGE reads at HPAGE_PMD_ORDER (512M) on arm64 with 64K pages, and suggested using a dynamic linker as an alternate approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
The force_thp_readahead path in do_sync_mmap_readahead() reads at
HPAGE_PMD_ORDER (2M on x86) and even doubles it to 4M for
non VM_RAND_READ mappings (ra->size *= 2), with async readahead
enabled. exec_folio_order() is more conservative. a single 2M folio
with async_size=0, no speculative prefetch. So I think the memory
pressure would not be worse than what x86 has?

For memory pressure on Android 16K: the readahead is clamped to VMA
boundaries, so a small shared library won't read 2M.
page_cache_ra_order() reduces folio order near EOF and on allocation
failure, so the 2M order is a preference, not a guarantee with the
current code?

---

I only started looking into how this works a few days before sending these
patches, so I could be wrong (please do correct me if thats the case!)

For the main binary (patch 3): load_elf_binary() controls load_bias.
Each PT_LOAD segment is mapped at load_bias + p_vaddr via elf_map().
The alignment variable feeds directly into load_bias calculation.
If p_vaddr=0 and p_offset=0, mapped_addr = load_bias + 0 = load_bias. By
ensuring load_bias is folio size aligned, the text segment's virtual address
is also folio size aligned.

For shared libraries (patch 4): ld.so loads these via mmap(), and the
kernel's get_unmapped_area callback (thp_get_unmapped_area for ext4,
xfs, btrfs) picks the virtual address. The existing code tries
PMD_SIZE alignment first (512M on 64K pages), which is too large for
typical shared libraries and always fails. Patch 4 adds a fallback
that tries folio-size alignment (2M), which is small enough to succeed
for most libraries.

---

On arm64 with 64K pages, the force_thp_readahead path triggered by
MADV_HUGEPAGE reads at HPAGE_PMD_ORDER (512M). Even with file and
anon khugepaged support aded for khugpaged, the collapse won't happen
form the start.

Yes I think dynamic linker is also a good alternate approach from Wangs
patches [1]. But doing it in the kernel would be more transparent?

[1] https://sourceware.org/pipermail/libc-alpha/2026-March/175776.html

review_comments.author: hev
review_comments.summary: The reviewer suggests moving the logic for determining whether a virtual address needs to be aligned to an exec folio size from the current location in the code to a separate function, maximum_alignment(), or even better, a new helper function should_align_to_exec_folio().
review_comments.raw_body:
I think this logic should live in maximum_alignment(), so we don't
have to walk the segments twice. It might be better to move it into a
separate helper, something like should_align_to_exec_folio()?

review_comments.author: WANG Rui
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that even when a user-space shared library's code segment satisfies PMD_SIZE (32MB), it may not end up at a PMD-aligned virtual address, and suggested skipping the PMD_SIZE == folio_sz case to avoid calling __thp_get_unmapped_area() twice with the same parameters.
review_comments.raw_body:
I noticed that even when the code segment of a user-space shared library
satisfies PMD_SIZE (32MB), it still doesn\u2019t end up at a PMD-aligned virtual
address. This might be the fallback you mentioned. Adjusting p_align in the
ld.so ELF loader does work, though it also avoids extremely large PMD_SIZE
values (capped at \u226432M).

It would probably be better to skip the PMD_SIZE == folio_sz case here,
so we don\u2019t end up calling __thp_get_unmapped_area() twice with the same
parameters.

Thanks,
Rui

review_comments.author: WANG Rui
review_comments.summary: Reviewer WANG Rui pointed out that the original patch's focus was on 64K normal pages, but they were actually referring to AArch64 with 4K normal pages. They provided measurement results showing that aligning code to PMD size (2M) performs slightly better than 64K for 4K pages.
review_comments.raw_body:
I only just realized your focus was on 64K normal pages, what I was
referring to here is AArch64 with 4K normal pages.

Sorry about the earlier numbers. They were a bit low precision.
RK3399 has pretty limited PMU events, and it looks like it can\u2019t
collect events from the A53 and A72 clusters at the same time, so
I reran the measurements on the A53.

Even though the A53 backend isn\u2019t very wide, we can still see the
impact from iTLB pressure. With 4K pages, aligning the code to PMD
size (2M) performs slightly better than 64K.

Binutils: 2.46
GCC: 15.2.1 (--enable-host-pie)

Workload: building vmlinux from Linux v7.0-rc1 with allnoconfig.
Loop: 5

                Base                 Patchset [1]         Patchset [2]
instructions    1,994,512,163,037    1,994,528,896,322    1,994,536,148,574
cpu-cycles      6,890,054,789,351    6,870,685,379,047    6,720,442,248,967
                                              ~ -0.28%             ~ -2.46%
itlb-misses           579,692,117          455,848,211           43,814,795
                                             ~ -21.36%            ~ -92.44%
time elapsed            1331.15 s            1325.50 s            1296.35 s
                                              ~ -0.42%             ~ -2.61%

Maybe we could make exec_folio_order() choose differently folio size
depending on the configuration and conditional in some way, for example
based on the size of the code segment?

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260310145406.3073394-1-usama.arif@linux.dev
[2] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/20260313005211.882831-1-r@hev.cc

Thanks,
Rui

review_comments.author: WANG Rui
review_comments.summary: Reviewer WANG Rui noted that most AArch64 and LoongArch64 binaries place the executable segment in the first PT_LOAD, satisfying alignment requirements for PMD-sized or large folio mappings, but this is not true for x86 due to linker defaults, specifically -z separate-code being the default on x86.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Arch Linux for AArch64 and LoongArch64 I've observed that most
binaries place the executable segment in the first PT_LOAD. In that
case both the virtual address and file offset are 0, which happens to
satisfy the alignment requirements for PMD-sized or large folio
mappings.

x86 looks quite different. The executable segment is usually not the
first one.

After digging into this I realized this mostly comes from the linker
defaults. With GNU ld, -z noseparate-code merges the read-only and
read-only-executable segments into one, while -z separate-code
splits them, placing a non-executable read-only segment first. The
latter is the default on x86, partly to avoid making the ELF headers
executable when mappings start from the beginning of the file.

Other architectures tend to default to -z noseparate-code, which
makes it more likely that the text segment is the first PT_LOAD.

LLVM lld behaves differently again: --rosegment (equivalent to
-z separate-code) is enabled by default on all architectures, which
similarly tends to place the executable segment after an initial
read-only one. That default is clearly less friendly to large-page
text mappings.

Thanks,
Rui
